coredata.dc:title: Development of fluorescence sensor and test paper based on molecularly imprinted carbon quantum dots for spiked detection of domoic acid in shellfish and lake water
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Linjie
Wen, Lejuan
Zhao, Liping
Chao, Jie
Tao, Feifei
Wang, Fei
Li, Caolong
coredata.dc:description: Excessive levels of domoic acid (DA) in edible shellfish and water can seriously threaten human health and even cause death. Herein, we have developed a fluorescence sensor and test paper based on molecularly imprinted carbon quantum dots (B-CDs@MIPs) for DA analysis. In this research, the prepared carbon quantum dots (B-CDs) with good optical properties were used as the sensitive fluorescent signal probes, and the molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) instead of biological antibodies were employed as the specific recognition components because of their high stability and low cost. Due to the shielding effect of imprinted silicon layer and the influence of modified groups introduced in the MIPs preparation process, the fluorescence signal of B-CDs in MIPs is inhibited. Interestingly, the DA can cause the partial recovery of the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs@MIPs by passivating the modified groups on the surface of B-CDs in MIPs. According to this principle, the designed fluorescence sensor reveals outstanding stability, super anti-interference ability, and excellent sensitivity, with the detection limit down to 10 nM. The sensor has been successfully applied to the rapid and accurate spiked analysis of DA in edible shellfish and lake water, providing excellent recoveries. More importantly, the designed inexpensive and easy-to-operate fluorescent test paper can realize preliminary qualitative analysis of DA on site by naked eye, indicating its promising application potential in food and environmental analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Domoic acid
Fluorescence sensor
Carbon quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Food and environmental analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and application of fluorescence test paper for DA analysis
Feasibility of DA detection by using B-CDs@MIPs
Fluorescence analysis of DA by fluorescence sensor
Fluorescent test paper for DA analysis
Comparison of our strategy with other reported method

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program ( 2019YFC0312602 ) as well as the Jiangsu Overseas Visiting Scholar Program for University Prominent Young & Middle-aged Teachers and Presidents.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of synthetized B-CDs. (B) The size distribution of B-CDs. (C) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of B-CDs. (D) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of B-CDs (the inset shows the physical photo under 365 nm UV lamp).

The SEM images of B-CDs@MIPs (A) and B-CDs@NIPs (B), respectively. (C) The FT-IR spectra of B-CDs@MIPs and B-CDs@NIPs. (D) The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of B-CDs@MIPs. (E) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of B-CDs@MIPs (the inset shows the physical photo under 365 nm UV lamp). (F) The fluorescence spectra of B-CDs@MIPs over the excitation wavelength range from 315 to 350 nm with an increment of 5 nm.
The fluorescence spectra of B-CDs@MIPs and B-CDs@NIPs with the absence or presence of DA.

(A) The effects of B-CDs@MIPs dosage on the fluorescence intensity of (F–F0) and (F–F0)/F0. (B) The effect of different incubation time on the F/F0. The impacts of pH (C) and temperature (D) on the fluorescence intensity of different systems and the F/F0, respectively. (F and F0 are the fluorescence values of B-CDs@MIPs with or without DA, respectively).

(A) The selectivity and interference tests of B-CDs@MIPs for DA detection (10 μM) with diverse substances (10 mg L−1). (B) The fluorescence spectra of fluorescence sensor with various concentrations of DA. (C) The dependency of the fluorescence ratio F/F0 on the concentration of DA. (D) The fitting curves between F/F0 and the content of DA.
(A) The effect of irradiation time under 365 nm UV lamp on the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs@MIPs. (B) The stability of the developed fluorescence sensor over time.
(A) The schematic diagram of fluorescent test paper for DA analysis. (B) The physical photos of fluorescent test paper toward DA with different levels (0–5 μM) under 365 nm UV lamp.
Route for synthesis of the B-CDs and B-CDs@MIPs (A) and analysis mechanism for DA (B).
Detection results of DA in lake water and shellfish samples (N = 3).

head.ce:title: Development of fluorescence sensor and test paper based on molecularly imprinted carbon quantum dots for spiked detection of domoic acid in shellfish and lake water

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors who equally contribute to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive levels of domoic acid (DA) in edible shellfish and water can seriously threaten human health and even cause death. Herein, we have developed a fluorescence sensor and test paper based on molecularly imprinted carbon quantum dots (B-CDs@MIPs) for DA analysis. In this research, the prepared carbon quantum dots (B-CDs) with good optical properties were used as the sensitive fluorescent signal probes, and the molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) instead of biological antibodies were employed as the specific recognition components because of their high stability and low cost. Due to the shielding effect of imprinted silicon layer and the influence of modified groups introduced in the MIPs preparation process, the fluorescence signal of B-CDs in MIPs is inhibited. Interestingly, the DA can cause the partial recovery of the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs@MIPs by passivating the modified groups on the surface of B-CDs in MIPs. According to this principle, the designed fluorescence sensor reveals outstanding stability, super anti-interference ability, and excellent sensitivity, with the detection limit down to 10 nM. The sensor has been successfully applied to the rapid and accurate spiked analysis of DA in edible shellfish and lake water, providing excellent recoveries. More importantly, the designed inexpensive and easy-to-operate fluorescent test paper can realize preliminary qualitative analysis of DA on site by naked eye, indicating its promising application potential in food and environmental analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Domoic acid (DA), a natural neurogenic amino acid, is mainly produced by some marine diatoms of genus pseudo-nitzschia and accumulated in shellfish [1,2]. Excessive DA intake can cause adverse symptoms such as memory loss, dizziness, coma, and even death [3–5]. The first poisoning death event caused by DA occurred on the east coast of Prince Edward Island, Canada in 1987 [6,7]. Since then, hazardous events by DA have been reported continuously on the coasts of Canada and the United States. Thus, Canada first established the maximum limit of DA in shellfish meat as 20 μg g−1, and then Europe and Japan have successively listed this toxin as a routine test item for shellfish [8,9].

In recent years, several regular methods have been developed to determine DA in shellfish and water, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10,11], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [12,13], mouse biological detection method (MBDE) [14], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [15,16], and high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [17,18] and so on. These technologies can offer extremely high accuracy and ultra-low detection limit. Unfortunately, the high detection cost and complex operation process limit their development in practical application. Recently, fluorescent sensors have aroused special interest due to their simple operation, low-cost, excellent performance, and visual readout, which are ideal option for rapid analysis of low-level marine toxins.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), a carbon-based zero-dimensional material with excellent optical properties, have attracted extensive attention of scientific researchers [19,20]. Recently, CDs have been extensively employed as ultra-sensitive fluorescent probes in the food and biochemical analysis because of their good water solubility, low toxicity, and inexpensive preparation cost [21–24]. Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) is a significant biomimetic recognition tool to simulate the specific recognition performance of antibodies and enzymes [25,26]. Therefore, the prepared molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) can not only exhibit the comparable selectivity and specificity with antibodies, but also compensate some defects of antibodies such as poor stability, difficult acquisition, and high price [27,28]. The combination of MIPs (identify components) and CDs (signal probes) can solve the problems of poor stability, low sensitivity, and poor selectivity of traditional fluorescence sensors based on CDs [29]. In recent years, the fluorescence sensors based on MIPs-CDs have been successfully developed and applied to the sensing of some analytes such as hemoglobin [30], perfluorooctane sulfonate [31], N-acyl homoserine lactones [32] because of its high selectivity and super sensitivity. Unfortunately, the fluorescence sensing strategy based on MIPs-CDs toward marine toxins, especially DA, are rarely reported.

In this work, we fabricated a fluorescence sensor and test paper based on MIPs-CDs for the specificity, visualization, and rapid analysis of DA in shellfish and lake water. As presented in Scheme 1 (A), the blue emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) with excellent optical properties were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method and employed as fluorescent signal probes. Meanwhile, the core components (B-CDs@MIPs) of fluorescence sensor were prepared by the combination of MIPs and CDs via a sol-gel strategy under the mild condition. Besides, the 1,3,5-pentanetrcarboxylicacid (PA) was selected as an appropriate dummy template to replace the target molecule on the basis of previous work owing to the high toxicity and expensive price of DA [33]. Obviously, the B-CDs@MIPs exhibit a clear emission peak at 440 nm under the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. However, the fluorescence signal of B-CDs in MIPs is inhibited owing to the shielding effect of imprinted silicon layer and the influence of modified groups introduced in the MIPs preparation process. Interestingly, DA can specifically bind to B-CDs@MIPs due to the high selectivity provided by MIT and passivate the surface groups of B-CDs in MIPs to partially recover the fluorescence signal of B-CDs@MIPs (Scheme 1(B)). Based on this strategy, rapid quantitative analysis of DA in shellfish and lake water samples with outstanding sensitivity and excellent selectivity is achieved. More attractively, the fluorescent test paper with perfect portability and simple operation has been developed and successfully applied to rapid preliminary qualitative analysis of DA on site by naked eye.

Citric acid (C6H8O7), urea (CH4N2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ammonia, 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTES), 1,3,5-pentanetrcarboxylicacid (PA), ethanol, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), methanol, glutamic acid (Glu), glutamine (Gln), ascorbic acid (AA), and acetic acid (HAc) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent. Cu2+, Mn2+, K+, and other ionic standard solutions were all provided by Guobiao (Beijing) Testing & Certification Co., Ltd. Domoic acid (DA) was acquired from National Research Council Canada. Unless otherwise stated, all these chemicals were directly employed without further purification. Ultrapure water was utilized throughout the study.

The fluorescence intensities were analyzed by Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Japan). The UV–vis absorption peak values and spectra were measured by Purkinje T6 spectrophotometer (China). Shellfish homogenate was acquired from a Wiggens D130 homogenizer (Germany). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were captured by a microscope (Supra 55, Carl Zeiss). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on a microscope (F20, FEI). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were gained by Varian 3100 FT-IR (USA) spectrometer.

B-CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method [34]. In detail, 210 mg citric acid and 100 mg urea were ultrasonically dissolved in 25 mL deionized water. Then the mixture solution was transferred into a 50 mL autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 4 h in the oven. After cooling to room temperature, the pH value of obtained material was adjusted to 7.0 by utilizing NaOH (1 M). Finally, the obtained B-CDs were diluted to 100 mL with ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C in the dark for further use.

B-CDs@MIPs was synthesized via a sol-gel strategy by utilizing TEOS as cross-linker, APTES as functional monomer, B-CDs as binding site, and PA as dummy template. Briefly, 3.0 mL B-CDs and 0.2 mL ammonia (25%) were first dispersed in 50 mL ethanol-water (1:2, V/V) and stirred for 10 min. Then, 0.1 mL APTES and 20.4 mg PA were added and stirred for another 30 min. After that, 1.0 mL TEOS (dispersed in 10 mL ethanol) was added dropwise to the above mixed solution and stirred overnight. Finally, the prepared product was collected by centrifugation.

To remove the dummy template molecule, the prepared product was eluted for several times with methanol/acetic acid (9:1, V/V) by centrifugal-ultrasonic circulation mode until the dummy template molecule could be not discovered by the UV–vis spectrophotometer. Then the product was washed with ultrapure water to remove residual methanol and acetic acid. Finally, the product was dispersed into 20 mL ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C for further use. Additionally, the non-imprinted carbon dots (B-CDs@NIPs) was synthesized in a completely consistent method except that no dummy template molecule was introduced.

Quantitative analysis of DA was conducted by Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer. In detail, 0.1 mL B-CDs@MIPs dispersion solution and 0.1 mL different concentrations of DA were mixed in a 5 mL plastic centrifuge tube, followed by the incubation for 10 min at room temperature. After that, 2.8 mL deionized water was added into the mixed solution and shaken evenly for fluorescence tests. The excitation wavelength was set to 340 nm and the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was measured. Finally, the relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) and the level of DA was investigated systematically. (F and F0 are the fluorescence values of B-CDs@MIPs with or without DA, respectively). The above experiments were all carried out three times.

To explore the selectivity of the developed fluorescence sensor towards DA, some possible co-existing interferents, including AA, Glu, Gln, CO3 2−, NO3−, Ac−, Cl−, PO4 3−, Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cr2+, Mn2+, K+, and Zn2+ were used to investigate. Additionally, the interference experiments for the mixture of DA and possible co-existing substances were performed under the same experimental conditions. The above experiments were all carried out three times.

Shellfish were purchased from the local seafood market, and the lake water was obtained from “Jing hu”, a lake on the campus of China Pharmaceutical University. Shellfish and lake water samples were analyzed for DA by spike and recovery test after a series of pretreatments. Briefly, 200 mL lake water was filtered with quantitative filter papers and 0.22 μm membranes to get rid of impurity substances and stored at room temperature. In addition, 50 g shellfish meat was first prepared into a homogenate by the homogenizer, and then 100 mL methanol/water (1:1, V/V) was added into the above homogenate and sonicated for 20 min. Afterwards, the supernatant was obtained by centrifugation, and the supernatant sample was filtered with quantitative filter papers and 0.22 μm membranes to get rid of impurity substances, followed by storing at 4 °C for further use.

Since the actual samples provided hardly contain DA, the experiments were carried out by spike and recovery test. In detail, a given level of DA solution was first prepared in pretreated lake water or shellfish. Subsequently, 0.1 mL the above spiked sample and 0.1 mL prepared B-CDs@MIPs dispersion solution were mixed in a 5 mL plastic centrifuge tube, and incubated for 10 min at room temperature. After that, 2.8 mL deionized water was added into the mixed solution and shaken evenly for fluorescence measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and application of fluorescence test paper for DA analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The test paper was prepared by using ordinary filter paper [35,36]. In brief, the filter paper was first cut into the discs with a diameter of 25 mm , and then 0.1 mL B-CDs@MIPs solution was dispersed evenly onto the surface of filter paper and dried naturally in the dark. The as-prepared test paper displayed blue fluorescence under a UV light at 365 nm. Subsequently, 0.1 mL DA solution with different levels (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, and 5.0 μM) were dipped onto the test papers. After incubation for 10 min, the fluorescence intensity responses of the test papers could be distinctly observed under a UV light at 365 nm.

The yellow-brown B-CDs solution was obtained by a simple hydrothermal method (Fig. S1) and characterized by several techniques. The morphology and particle size of synthesized B-CDs is displayed by TEM, and the corresponding TEM image is depicted in Fig. 1 A. The TEM demonstrates the uniformly distributed dot-like particle morphology of B-CDs, and their average particle size is about 3.5 nm (Fig. 1B). As depicted in Fig. 1C, the UV–vis spectrum expresses a distinct peak at 339 nm, which can be attributed to the n-π∗ transition of CO and other surface moieties [37]. The fluorescence test reveals that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of B-CDs are located at 370 and 440 nm, respectively, and the B-CDs solution emits intensive blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 1D). Additionally, the emission fluorescence intensity of B-CDs is highly dependent on the excitation light used. As depicted in Fig. S2, the optimal excitation wavelength is 370 nm and emission signal decreases gradually as the excitation wavelength deviates from the optimal value. However, the emission peak reveals a negligible shift with the excitation wavelength range of 350–390 nm, which may be caused by nitrogen doping [38].

B-CDs@MIPs was synthesized via a sol-gel strategy under the mild condition, and the prepared product was characterized by several characterization instruments. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, the prepared B-CDs@MIPs presents a spherical morphology with a diameter ranging from 100 to 500 nm, and the rough surface imprinted layer can be clearly observed (Fig. S3). As expected, B-CDs@NIPs reveals similar micromorphology to B-CDs@MIPs (Fig. 2B and Fig. S4). The surface groups of prepared B-CDs@MIPs and B-CDs@NIPs were analyzed by FT-IR. As displayed in Fig. 2C, the FT-IR peaks at 460 and 785 cm−1 are ascribed to the Si–O vibrations and the distinct characteristic peak at 1055 cm−1 is attributed to asymmetric Si–O–Si stretching, confirming the existence of SiO2 matrices. Additionally, the FT-IR peaks at 1560 and 3420 cm−1 are N–H stretching vibration and the peak at 2930 cm−1 is C–H stretching vibration, indicating the existence of APTES in B-CDs@MIPs [39,40]. Besides, the FT-IR spectra of B-CDs@MIPs and B-CDs@NIPs are extremely similar, indicating their similar chemical composition and surface groups.

To study the optical performances of prepared product, the UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometers were employed. As depicted in Fig. 2D, compared with the B-CDs (Fig. 1C), the UV–Vis absorption peak of B-CDs@MIPs exhibits slightly movement, which may be caused by MIPs on the surface of B-CDs. Moreover, the UV–Vis characteristic peaks of B-CDs@MIPs and B-CDs@NIPs are virtually identical (Fig. S5), further indicating their similar composition and structure. As depicted in Figs. 1D and 2E, and Fig. S6, the optimal emission wavelengths of the three are the consistent, while the optimal excitation wavelengths of B-CDs@MIPs and B-CDs@NIPs have varying degree of blue shift compared with that of B-CDs. This phenomenon demonstrates that the imprinted layer and dummy template have impacts on the optimal excitation peak of B-CDs. However, compared with B-CDs, the fluorescence signal of B-CDs@MIPs is significantly reduced under the same instrument parameters (Fig. S7). In addition, the emission fluorescence intensity and peak position of imprinted materials are highly correlated with the excitation light used. As the excitation wavelength deviated from the optimal value, the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs@MIPs and B-CDs@NIPs gradually decrease and the emission peaks shift slightly (Fig. 2F and Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of DA detection by using B-CDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs are carbon-based zero-dimensional materials with excellent optical properties [41]. However, the luminescence behavior of CDs cannot be well explained by a unified theory so far. Currently, the proposed photoluminescence mechanisms include quantum confinement effect, surface state, molecular state, conjugation effect, synergistic effect, environment effect, crosslink-enhanced emission and so on [42]. According to previous reports, the first three mechanisms are accepted by most researchers. As for the surface state of CDs, which plays a more significant part in the luminescence behavior of CDs rather than the intrinsic core state. For instance, the existence of defect states, some traps on the surface of CDs and non-radiative relaxation procedures can decrease fluorescence signal because the excited electrons also fall to the ground state in the absence of photons generation. Specifically, certain groups on the surface of CDs, such as alkylamines and carbonyl groups, can trigger the non-radiative recombination, thereby reducing the luminous efficiency of CDs. Therefore, passivation or reduction of these groups can recover the fluorescence signal of CDs [43,44].

According to the result of Fig. S7, it is obvious that the fluorescence signal of B-CDs@MIPs is greatly reduced relative to that of B-CDs. This phenomenon may be caused by the following reasons: The B-CDs@MIPs was fabricated by coating B-CDs with an imprinted silicon layer via a sol-gel strategy, and the thicker molecular imprinting layer can mask the fluorescence of the inner B-CDs, resulting in the weakening of the fluorescence signal of B-CDs@MIPs [45,46]. Meanwhile, the alkylamine group of APTES that is the functional monomer in the MIPs preparation process can induce the non-radiative of the excited electrons in B-CDs, which also cause the fluorescence signal of B-CDs@MIPs to weaken. However, the fluorescent signal of B-CDs in the MIPs is partially recovered in the presence of DA (Fig. 3 ). This phenomenon is because the alkylamine group of APTES in the MIPs can be passivated by connecting to DA, resulting in the partial recovery of fluorescence signal of B-CDs in the MIPs by reducing the non-radiative recombination [47–49]. Nevertheless, the fluorescence signal of B-CDs@NIPs exhibits slightly recovery in the presence of DA (Fig. 3). This is because DA can't be specifically adsorbed and captured in large amounts owing to the absence of molecularly imprinted cavities on the B-CDs@NIPs. However, a very small amount of DA is combined with APTES by nonspecific adhesion to B-CDs@NIPs, resulting in slight recovery of fluorescence signal. These results confirm that the developed fluorescence sensor based on B-CDs@MIPs can be used for the sensing of DA.

With the above principle, we further adopted the designed fluorescence sensor based on B-CDs@MIPs for quantitative analysis of DA. To achieve better detection performance, several experimental parameters such as the dosage of B-CDs@MIPs, incubation time, pH value, and temperature were optimized. As displayed in Fig. 4 A, the dosage of B-CDs@MIPs employed is closely related to fluorescence enhancement efficiency ((F–F0)/F0), which is crucial to improve the sensitivity of quantitative analysis and linear range of the fluorescence sensor [50]. With the increase of B-CDs@MIPs dosage, the fluorescence enhancement efficiency gradually increases and reaches the maximum at 100 μL (Fig. 4A). Therefore, 100 μL B-CDs@MIPs is employed as the optimal dosage for the sensor. To guarantee sufficient binding between DA and the specific recognition sites on B-CDs@MIPs, the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs@MIPs + DA system with different incubation time were measured to explore the optimal incubation time. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) rapidly increases within 10 min and then reaches equilibrium. Therefore, the incubation time is set to 10 min in the following experiments unless otherwise specified.

To explore the optimal pH value of analysis system, B-CDs@MIPs was placed in the different pH conditions from 3.0 to 9.0 to measure fluorescence intensity with or without DA. As displayed in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs@MIPs is hardly affected by pH, suggesting that B-CDs@MIPs has outstanding stability in a wide pH range. Besides, the B-CDs@MIPs + DA system reveals undifferentiated fluorescence intensity and the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) under acidic and neutral conditions, but decrease under alkaline conditions. To choose an optimal pH condition and reduce experiment interference, nearly neutral deionized water is employed as the reaction solvent in this study.

Subsequently, B-CDs@MIPs with or without DA were placed in the different temperature conditions from 5 to 55 °C to explore the influence of temperature. As shown in Fig. 4D, the temperature exhibits a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs@MIPs. However, excessive temperature can slightly reduce the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs@MIPs + DA system. Accordingly, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) decreases when the temperature is above 35 °C. Consequently, the experiments are all carried out at room temperature (25 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of DA by fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the selectivity and anti-interference of the developed fluorescence sensor, some possible co-existing interferents including AA, Glu, Gln, CO3 2−, NO3 −, Ac−, Cl−, PO4 3−, Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cr2+, Mn2+, K+, and Zn2+ were used in this test. As depicted in Fig. 5 A, these interferents present negligible fluorescence response towards B-CDs@MIPs, indicating that the designed sensor exhibits excellent selectivity to DA. When each interferent coexists with DA, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) displays negligible changes compared with DA alone. These results suggest the possible co-existing interferents have no obvious impact on the B-CDs@MIPs + DA system. Thus, the developed fluorescence sensor can provide outstanding selectivity and specificity for DA detection thanks to MIT.

Subsequently, the developed fluorescence sensor combining MIPs (recognition element) and CDs (signal probe) was used for quantitative detection of DA under optimal experimental conditions. As presented in Fig. 5B and C, the fluorescence intensity and the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of the incubation system gradually increases with the increase of DA. Further, the F/F0 is well linear with logarithm of DA concentration, offering the equations: Y = 0.178X + 1.601 (R 2 = 0.994) (0.05–5 μM). According to the signal-to-noise of three (S/N = 3), the limit of detection (LOD) towards DA is calculated as low as 10 nM.

To verify the reliability of the developed fluorescence sensor in practical application, the shellfish and lake water samples were spiked for DA detection. Considering matrix effect in the actual sample, it is essential to explore the effect of ionic strength on the detection of DA in this study. As shown in Fig. S9, the ionic strength has almost no effect on the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) when the concentration of the salt solution is below 0.15 mol L−1. Additionally, the little influence of ionic strength on the F/F0 has been observed, even under the condition of high-salt concentration.

Subsequently, we purchased shellfish samples from the local seafood market, and obtained the lake water from “Jing hu” on the campus of China Pharmaceutical University. After pretreatment (Section 2.5), the DA spiked in the shellfish and lake water samples have been analyzed by the developed fluorescence sensor. As displayed in Table 1 , our proposed fluorescence strategy can provide excellent recoveries (95%–105%) for quantitative analysis of DA in spiked samples, suggesting its outstanding reliability for DA detection in actual samples. Therefore, these results indicate that the proposed sensor has potential practical application prospects in food analysis.

Optical stability is the crucial performance for fluorescence sensors [51,52]. Thus, the stability of designed fluorescence sensor was investigated by testing the fluorescence intensity changes against irradiation time under 365 nm UV lamp and storage time under suitable environment. As displayed in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs@MIPs has no obvious difference under 365 nm UV light for an hour, indicating that its good photobleaching resistance. In addition, the fluorescence intensity and fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of developed fluorescence sensor can remain almost unchanged when B-CDs@MIPs is stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 30 days (Fig. 6B). These results illustrate that the proposed sensor has outstanding optical stability, certifying its great potential for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent test paper for DA analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze DA more conveniently, we have designed a fluorescence test paper based on B-CDs@MIPs for rapid preliminary qualitative analysis of DA on site within 20 min. As displayed in Fig. 7 A, the fluorescence test paper is prepared by dropping B-CDs@MIPs evenly onto ordinary filter paper, which displays blue fluorescence under a UV lamp at 365 nm. After addition of DA and incubating for 10 min, the fluorescence of the test paper becomes brighter. However, the fluorescence cannot be observed under 365 nm UV lamp when only DA or water is dispersed evenly onto the blank filter paper (Fig. S10). As depicted in Fig. 7B, the fluorescent test paper gets brighter and brighter with the increase of DA level, showing a visible change to the naked eye. This result indicates that test paper can be used for rapid preliminary qualitative detection of DA by naked eye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of our strategy with other reported method
ce:section.ce:para: The contrast of our proposed fluorescence sensing strategy with other previously reported methods was listed in Table S1 (Supporting Information) [53–57]. Obviously, our fluorescence sensing strategy is comparable to some reported methods in terms of linear range and LOD. However, the designed fluorescence sensor with excellent sensitivity only utilizes inexpensive fluorescent probe, which is attributed to CDs with low-cost and excellent optical properties. In terms of recognition components, the introduction of MIT instead of expensive antibodies can also endow designed sensor with excellent selectivity and outstanding specificity, while also greatly reducing the cost for DA detection. More attractively, the sensor additionally exhibits superiorities of good optical stability, superior anti-interference ability, and simple operation process. It is worth mentioning that developed inexpensive and easy-to-operate fluorescent test paper can achieve rapid preliminary qualitative analysis of DA by naked eye, which emerges potential practical value for portable monitoring of DA.

In short, a facile but effective fluorescence sensor based on B-CDs@MIPs has been developed and successfully applied to the detection of DA in lake water and shellfish samples. The sensor has exhibited excellent sensitivity for selective recognition and rapid analysis of DA with a wide linear range and low LOD, which is attributed to low-cost CDs with good optical properties and classic MIT. More surprisingly, the prepared inexpensive and easy-to-operate fluorescence test paper makes instrument-free on-site preliminary qualitative analysis of DA possible, which exhibit great application potential in food and environmental analysis.

Linjie Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Lejuan Wen: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Liping Zhao: Formal analysis, Validation. Jie Chao: Resources. Feifei Tao: Resources. Fei Wang: Visualization, Supervision. Caolong Li: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program (2019YFC0312602) as well as the Jiangsu Overseas Visiting Scholar Program for University Prominent Young & Middle-aged Teachers and Presidents.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2022.339515.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effective extraction of domoic acid from seafood based on postsynthetic-modified magnetic zeolite imidazolate framework-8 particles
